  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Has COVID Really Reduced Air Pollution in India?

2020-07-06

The Context

As lockdowns around the world continue to grind on, we are starting to notice their effects on all aspects of life. One of the rare silver linings that has emerged in popular discussion is the dramatic reduction in air pollution that lockdowns have caused. Research on the original Wuhan lockdown suggested that the resulting declines in air pollution (63% reduction in nitrogen dioxide) may have saved up to 11,000 lives in China.

Similar analyses have been carried out in India, with headlines like “Air pollution dropped significantly during lockdown” and conclusions like “India’s nationwide lockdown, in particular, has had stunning effects on air pollution levels.” But reading the language used in these articles made me nervous:

“Air pollution dropped significantly during 74-day lockdown period.” (title)

“The clampdown on all non-essential activities due to the COVID-19 pandemic, from March 25 to June 8, led to a significant decline in air pollution levels for major cities across India.” (subtitle)

The title and the subtitle are saying two different things! Just because air pollution dropped during the lockdown doesn’t mean that the lockdown caused a significant decline in air pollution levels. It’s the old saw: correlation doesn’t mean causation. So I wanted to look into some air quality data myself to see the story. The fact that I’m writing about it must mean that something is off about this causal story.

The Analysis

I obtained air quality index (AQI) data from this popular dataset, which extracts air quality data from the Central Pollution Control Board’s data platform. In particular, I used daily city-level data from 2015-2020, focusing on four major cities: Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata and Bangalore. I chose these cities because they are the biggest cities, in which industrial and economic activity are likely to be the biggest sources of pollution - and because the Indian Express article that prompted this investigation focuses on them.

set.seed(20200706)
library(pacman)
p_load("dplyr", "ggplot2", "gridExtra", "zoo", "ggthemes")
theme_set(theme_wsj())

cities <- c("Delhi", "Mumbai", "Kolkata", "Bengaluru")
recent <- read.csv("./city_recent.csv") 
aqi <- read.csv("./city_day.csv") %>%
  bind_rows(recent) %>% 
  filter(City %in% cities) %>%
  mutate(Date = as.Date(Date)) %>%
  arrange(City, Date) %>% 
  mutate(AQI_MA7 = rollapplyr(AQI, 7, mean, fill=NA))

The first investigative question is, how has the AQI in each city changed since the lockdown began?

aqi %>%
  filter(Date >= "2020-03-25") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Date, y = AQI_MA7, color = City)) + 
  geom_line() + 
  geom_smooth(method = 'lm') + 
  facet_wrap(~ City) +
  labs(y = "7-day moving average of AQI") + 
  theme(legend.position = "none")

This already doesn’t seem to bear out the conclusion that air quality has dramatically increased during the lockdown period - it has improved slightly in general, and actually gotten worse in Delhi. The most likely reason for this difference is because I’m focusing on AQI, whereas the article focuses on the decline in specific pollutants like NO2. With the caveat that I am not an environmental scientist, focusing on AQI seems to be more sound:

  1. It’s calculated in a defined way set out by the CPCB, so there’s no risk of a misleading conclusion from selectively looking at some pollutant rather than others that tell a more favorable story.

  2. CPCB guidelines correspond to AQI levels and give health impacts corresponding to each AQI level, which can be more easily applied than aggregating impacts corresponding to each specific pollutant level.

The story doesn’t get much better when looking at my main concern, which was how the AQI decline post-lockdown compares to the AQI-decline pre-lockdown.

aqi %>% 
  filter(Date > "2020-01-01") %>%
  mutate(Post = ifelse(Date >= "2020-03-25", 1, 0)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Date, y = AQI_MA7, color = City, group = Post)) + 
  geom_line()  + 
  geom_smooth(method = 'lm') + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(as.Date("2020-03-25")), linetype=4) + 
  facet_wrap(~City) + 
  labs(y = "7-day moving average of AQI") + 
  theme(legend.position = "none")

It turns out that in all four cities, AQI was on a steady decline throughout 2020 in the pre-lockdown period. It is interesting to note that there seems to be a break at the beginning of lockdown, March 25th. But that should be taken with a grain of salt: after all, if you generate an arbitrary date to separate periods in 2020, the lines of best fit will often have a break. The next cell allows you to simulate this yourself.

random_break <- sample(as.numeric(as.Date("2020-01-01")):as.numeric(as.Date("2020-07-06")), 1)
aqi %>% 
  filter(Date > "2020-01-01") %>%
  mutate(Post = ifelse(Date >= as.Date(random_break), 1, 0)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Date, y = AQI_MA7, color = City, group = Post)) + 
  geom_line()  + 
  geom_smooth(method = 'lm') + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = random_break, linetype=4) + 
  facet_wrap(~City) + 
  labs(y = "7-day moving average of AQI") + 
  theme(legend.position = "none")

When we rewind to 2019, it becomes clear that the decline in AQI in 2020 is part of a seasonal effect that we can see in full cyclical form in 2019.

aqi %>% 
  filter(Date > "2019-01-01") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Date, y = AQI_MA7, color = City)) + 
  geom_line() + 
  geom_smooth(method = 'lm') + 
  facet_wrap(~City) + 
  labs(y = "7-day moving average of AQI") + 
  theme(legend.position = "none")

It seems that air quality has a strong seasonal pattern, and focusing only on 2020 misses that seasonal pattern. There has been a general trend towards better air quality (lower AQI),

However, this isn’t quite an appropriate comparison: perhaps the trend towards better air quality has been accelerated by lockdown? To study this, we can compare AQI on a given day in 2020 to AQI a year ago. If that diverges after lockdown, then it suggests that lockdown is affecting this trend.

aqi %>% 
Warning messages:
1: In readChar(file, size, TRUE) : truncating string with embedded nuls
2: In readChar(file, size, TRUE) : truncating string with embedded nuls
3: In readChar(file, size, TRUE) : truncating string with embedded nuls
4: In readChar(file, size, TRUE) : truncating string with embedded nuls
5: In readChar(file, size, TRUE) : truncating string with embedded nuls
  mutate(AQI_before = lag(AQI, 365), 
         AQI_dif = AQI_before - AQI,
         AQI_dif_MA7 = rollapplyr(AQI_dif, 15, mean, fill=NA),
         Post = ifelse(Date > "2020-03-25", 1, 0)) %>% 
  filter(Date >= "2020-01-01") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Date, y = AQI_dif_MA7, color = City, group = Post)) + 
  geom_line() + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(as.Date("2020-03-25")), linetype=4) + 
  facet_wrap(~City) + 
  labs(title = "AQI decrease from 365 days ago") + 
  theme(legend.position = "none")


# aqi20 <- aqi %>% filter(Date >= "2020-01-01") %>% mutate(Time = as.numeric(Date) - 18262)
# summary(lm(AQI ~ Time + Post, aqi20))

The Interpretation

I’m more than a little troubled by these results. They can only be rationalized in one of two ways:

  1. India’s urban lockdowns do not target the sources of air pollution.
  2. India’s urban lockdowns are not enforced enough to reduce polluting activity.

sfdsdfsdf

Regardless of the substantive takeaway, this presents a warning to using air quality in short-term analyses like COVID-19 effects: air quality is highly volatile on a day-to-day basis.
